name: Monstera deliciosa
aboutText: Monstera deliciosa is the plant that introduced an entire generation to tropical foliage — a robust hemiepiphytic climber from the humid forests of southern Mexico to Panama that has become one of the most recognised houseplants on earth. In its juvenile stage the leaves are entire and cordate, clinging to trunks and working upward; as the plant matures and climbs into brighter canopy positions, the foliage transforms dramatically into the large, leathery, deeply split blades with characteristic inner fenestrations that have made the species so visually iconic. Stout, geniculate petioles project the broad mature leaves outward from the support with considerable authority. The base species remains a worthwhile addition to any collection in its own right — large, bold, architecturally significant, and considerably more forgiving than many of its rarer cousins. A gateway plant that earns its place in the most advanced collections too.

morphology.leafShape: Juvenile: entire cordate; Mature: deeply pinnately split with inner fenestrations
morphology.venation: Bold midrib with pinnate lateral veins between sinuses and fenestrations
morphology.growthHabit: Strong climber with thick stems, conspicuous nodes, and robust aerial roots

careGuide.substrate: Chunky, well-draining mix: 40% potting compost, 30% perlite, 20% orchid bark, 10% worm castings. Monsteras are more tolerant of moisture than most aroids but still need drainage.
careGuide.watering: Water when the top 3–5 cm is dry. These are forgiving plants but still susceptible to root rot if kept too wet. Water less in winter.
careGuide.humidity: 50–70% preferred. More tolerant of typical UK indoor humidity than other aroids, though 60%+ encourages larger leaf development.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced fertiliser at half strength every 2 weeks from spring through summer. Monthly in winter or none at all.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months when roots emerge from drainage holes. Provide a moss pole or support — climbing encourages larger leaf production.

commonProblems.cause: Plant is juvenile, or insufficient light
commonProblems.fix: Ensure bright indirect light and allow the plant to mature — fenestrations develop with age and better conditions
Check soil moisture before watering; begin a regular fertilising routine in growing season

commonProblems.cause: Sunburn or cold water on leaves
commonProblems.fix: Avoid direct sun; water at the base and ensure water is room temperature

items: Ensure there is at least one healthy node present — a leaf without a node cannot produce new growth
For variegated forms, verify variegation is present on the newest leaf and is not reverting to green
Check for scale insects on petioles and undersides of leaves — monsteras are susceptible
Confirm the cutting or plant has been rooted before purchasing, unless you are buying intentionally unrooted — unrooted cuttings require a propagation setup to succeed

fieldNotes.title: The One Everyone Has
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: I will be fair: Monstera deliciosa is a genuinely good plant. The mature fenestrated leaf — those elegant splits and perforations first described by Liebmann from Mexican specimens in 1849 — is a piece of natural design that holds up regardless of how ubiquitous it has become. The Victorian plant hunters who first encountered it in Mexican cloud forest were not wrong to be impressed. What it has since become is a social media phenomenon that somewhat escapes its actual botanical merits. A large mature specimen with full fenestration commands serious prices and earns them. The small, leaf-on-coffee-table version that never fenestrates is what most British buyers are actually purchasing. These are, practically speaking, different experiences.
